title: Pivoting colloidal assemblies exhibit mechanical metamaterial behaviour
author: Julio Melio
fullText:
Data availability
 
 The data that support the plots in this paper are available from Zenodo at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.14886689 (ref. 45).
 Code availability
 
 The models supporting the findings of this study are described in the paper and its Supplementary Information. The code that supports the findings of this study is available from Zenodo at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.14886689 (ref. 45).

 Additional informationPublisher’s note Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.Extended data figures and tablesExtended Data Fig. 1 DNA functionalization scheme.a, Interaction matrix of particles functionalized with dsDNA strands A, A′, B and B′ as indicated. Particles have been functionalized with linkers AB (green), A′B (purple) and B′ (yellow) such that they can form bonds between their complementary strands, indicated by primes. b, This interaction matrix makes it possible to create mechanically stable triangular-shaped and diamond-shaped units in colloidal pivots using the depicted design.Extended Data Fig. 2 Design principles of rotating diamond structures.a, Schematics of rotating diamond lattices with particles of size rs and rb with angles θ1, θ2 and α indicated. b, Monodisperse particles (that is, for rs/rb = 1) lead to hexagonally closed packed structures and rigidity. c, The structure also rigidifies for the lower size limit, that is, for \({r}_{{\rm{s}}}=(\sqrt{2}-1){r}_{{\rm{b}}}\). d, Because a rotating diamond structure is a mechanism, the angles θ1 and θ2 are anticorrelated, that is, when θ1 = θmin, θ2 = θmax and vice versa. e, The angular range of motion θmax − θmin that is allowed without particle overlap has an optimum at rs = 0.637rb.Extended Data Fig. 3 Triangular and diamond pivots.a,d, Particle positions with translation and rotation subtracted. b,e, Probability density function (PDF) of the opening angle θ as defined in Fig. 1d,e, respectively. c,f, Free energy calculated from the PDF by (F − F0)/(kBT) = −ln(p), in which F0 is chosen to be the minimum of F. a–c show data for the triangular-shaped and d–f for the diamond-shaped pivots.Extended Data Fig. 4 Angle correlations of rotating diamond structure.a–h, Experimentally measured angle correlations of a rotating diamond structure with N = 18 between the coloured angles indicated in a are plotted in b–h. For θ3, θ4, θ5 and θ6, the average of the two opposing indicated angles is calculated and used. The solid black lines represent linear fits with θi = aθ1 + b, with a the slope, whose value is noted in each panel and close to 1 or −1. i,j, Particle bonds used to calculate the variation in bond length Δr with respect to the mean value ⟨r⟩, for which the mean probability density distributions are plotted in j. The bonds indicated in black in i were not used owing to a small but noticeable tracking error. Bonds between larger/smaller particles are shown in purple/green, respectively. The shaded region in j represents the standard deviation.Extended Data Fig. 5 Mode spectra for rotating diamond structures.a–d, Mode spectra for experiments (a), Brownian particle simulations (b), a Monte Carlo-based conformation sampling approach (c) and a linear spring model of rotating diamond structures with N = 8, 13 and 18, as indicated by the colour (d). The mode stiffnesses are the eigenvalues κν obtained from the stiffness matrices (Methods). Experiments and simulations show one mode that is much softer than the other modes, as exemplified by a large jump in stiffness, which is expected from the network structure. Modes obtained from conformation sampling reveal two soft modes that match the softest experimental and simulation modes. Because of a rigid bond assumption, stiff modes cannot be captured. The modes obtained from the linear spring model, which captures extension and compression of bonds, matches well with the stiff modes of the experimental data. The soft modes are strictly zero and hence not visible in the plot. The shaded region in a represents the standard deviation.Extended Data Fig. 6 Mode spectra for kagome structures.a–d, Mode spectra for experiments (a), Brownian particle simulations (b), a Monte Carlo-based conformation sampling approach (c) and a linear spring model of kagome structures with N = 12, 19 and 24 particles as indicated by the colour (d). In experiments and simulations, three, five and six much softer modes are found, as exemplified by a jump in stiffness, matching expectations for the number of soft modes based on the bond network. Note that experimental resolution limits the stiffness values visible in experiment. Modes obtained from sampling all possible conformations describe soft reconfigurations only and shows the nonlinear effects arising from large soft displacements also present in experiments and simulations. The modes obtained from the linear response model match the stiffness of the stiffer modes well. The soft modes are strictly zero and hence not visible in the plot. The shaded region in a represents the standard deviation.Extended Data Fig. 7 Experimental data of a five-ring kagome structure with N = 34 particles.a, Bright-field snapshot. b, Translation-corrected and rotation-corrected particle positions, with the rigid triangular units indicated in blue. c, Mean particle positions with bond network drawn and the two deviating interactions indicated. d,e, Two representative opening angles and their time traces. f,g, Rigid unit angle definition and their range. h,i, Opening angle definition and their range. j,k, Neighbouring particle distances and their range. l,m, Next-nearest-neighbour distances on the same side of the pivot and their range. n,o, Diagonal next-nearest-neighbour distance and their range. Data ranges are plotted for all angles or distances occurring in the structure. Scale bar, 10 μm.Extended Data Fig. 8 Experimental data of a six-ring kagome structure with N = 39 particles.a, Bright-field snapshot. b, Translation-corrected and rotation-corrected particle positions, with the rigid units indicated in blue. c, Mean particle positions with bond network drawn and three deviating interactions indicated. d,e, Two representative opening angles and their time traces. f,g, Rigid unit angle definition and their range. h,i, Opening angle definition and their range. j,k, Neighbouring particle distance definition and their range. l,m, Next-nearest-neighbour distances on the same side of the unit and their range. n,o, Diagonal next-nearest-neighbour distance definition and their range. Data ranges are plotted for all angles or distances occurring in the structure. Scale bar, 10 μm.Extended Data Fig. 9 Magnetic kagome structures.a, When the rotation frequency of the magnetic field is higher than the response time of the structure, the particles experience an attractive force ⟨F⟩t. b,c, Schematic (b) and bright-field snapshots (c) of small kagome structures made from all magnetic particles before (left panels) and after (right panels) applying a rotating magnetic field of 10 Hz. The structure folds into two different conformations. d, The potential energy calculated as the sum of all dipole interactions shows eight minima when only magnetic particles are used. e,f, Schematic (e) and bright-field microscopy snapshots (f). On application of a rotating magnetic field of 10 Hz (f, right panel), the structure folds into a unique conformation. g, Potential energy of a kagome structure made from three magnetic (darker, slightly bigger) and nine non-magnetic (lighter, slightly smaller) particles. The potential has a single minimum. h, Radius of gyration of a kagome structure with all particles being magnetic with conformations at specific times indicated by colour. A rotating magnetic field is applied at times shaded in purple, followed by clear drops in the radius of gyration corresponding to a folding of the structure. Scale bars, 5 μm.Extended Data Table 1 Overview of ssDNA strand sequencesFull size tableSupplementary informationSupplementary MethodsThis file contains an optimization strategy to select the particle size ratio resulting in the largest angular range of motion in rotating diamond structures, a discussion on the angle (anti)correlations observed in experiments of rotating diamond structures, the particle position vector for the undeformed rotating diamond and kagome structures, a position vector parametrization using opening angles, information on the quantity of video data and an interpretation of the mode spectra.Peer Review FileSupplementary Video 1This video demonstrates the thermal motion of flexible colloidal pivots. In the first minute, a pivot consisting of rigid triangular units is shown and in the second minute, a pivot consisting of diamond-shaped units is shown. On the right, the bright-field microscopy video is overlaid with blue polygons indicating the rigid units. Both structures show pivoting motion around a central particle. The videos are sped up 10× from real time and the scale bars are 2 μm (first pivot) and 4 μm (second pivot).Supplementary Video 2This video shows a colloidal realization of a rotating diamond structure with N = 8 that moves owing to thermal fluctuations. On the right, the bright-field microscopy video is overlaid with blue polygons indicating the rigid units. Particles with radii rb = 1.67 μm and rs = 1.06 μm were used. The video is sped up 10× from real time and the scale bar is 5 μm.Supplementary Video 3This video shows a colloidal realization of a rotating diamond structure with N = 13 that moves owing to thermal fluctuations. On the right, the bright-field microscopy video is overlaid with blue polygons indicating the rigid units. The video is sped up 10× from real time and the scale bar is 5 μm.Supplementary Video 4This video shows a colloidal realization of a rotating diamond structure with N = 18 that moves owing to thermal fluctuations. On the right, the bright-field microscopy video is overlaid with blue polygons indicating the rigid units. The video is sped up 10× from real time and the scale bar is 5 μm.Supplementary Video 5This video shows a colloidal realization of a rotating triangle, or kagome, structure with N = 12 that moves owing to thermal fluctuations. On the right, the bright-field microscopy video is overlaid with blue polygons indicating the rigid units. Particles with a radius of 1.06 μm were used. The video is sped up 10× from real time and the scale bar is 5 μm.Supplementary Video 6This video shows a colloidal realization of a rotating triangle, or kagome, structure with N = 19 that moves owing to thermal fluctuations. On the right, the bright-field microscopy video is overlaid with blue polygons indicating the rigid units. The video is sped up 10× from real time and the scale bar is 5 μm.Supplementary Video 7This video shows a colloidal realization of a rotating triangle, or kagome, structure with N = 24 that moves owing to thermal fluctuations. On the right, the bright-field microscopy video is overlaid with blue polygons indicating the rigid units. The video is sped up 10× from real time and the scale bar is 5 μm.Supplementary Video 8This video shows a kagome structure with N = 12 consisting of superparamagnetic particles. On applying an in-plane fast-rotating magnetic field of 10 Hz, particles attract and two compact conformations are observed. On the right, the bright-field microscopy video is overlaid with blue polygons indicating the rigid units. The video is sped up 10× from real time and the scale bar is 4 μm.Supplementary Video 9This video shows a kagome structure with N = 12 consisting of three superparamagnetic particles and nine non-magnetic particles. On the right, the bright-field microscopy video is overlaid with blue polygons indicating the rigid units. On applying an in-plane fast-rotating magnetic field of 10 Hz, the superparamagnetic particles attract and a compact conformation is observed with all magnetic particles on the inside of the structure. On removing the magnetic field, the structure unfolds by thermal fluctuations. The video is sped up 10× from real time and the scale bar is 5 μm.Supplementary Video 10This video shows a colloidal realization of a rotating triangle, or kagome, structure with N = 34 that moves owing to thermal fluctuations. On the right, the bright-field microscopy video is overlaid with blue polygons indicating the rigid units. The video is sped up 10× from real time and the scale bar is 5 μm.Supplementary Video 11This video shows a colloidal realization of a rotating triangle, or kagome, structure with N = 39 that moves owing to thermal fluctuations. On the right, the bright-field microscopy video is overlaid with blue polygons indicating the rigid units.
